## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, then draw the high-level design one flow at a time — onboarding, search, order+payment, delivery+tracking, notifications — and then go deep on the two or three hardest parts: the location firehose, the matching engine, and not double-charging anyone. I'll check in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension in a food-delivery platform: it's **three different systems wearing one app** — a *read-heavy discovery engine* (browse/search restaurants), a *money-correct transaction system* (place order, pay, don't double-charge), and a *real-time geo system* (assign a rider, stream their dot on a map). Each wants a different database and a different consistency stance, and the whole thing peaks hard at lunch and dinner. I'll target an **Amazon-style bar** — cost and operations are first-class, and I'll work backwards from the customer (hungry user + restaurant + rider). Tell me if you'd rather I tilt elsewhere.

### Functional (the core flows — "above the line")

**1. Restaurant onboarding & menu management** — A restaurant should be able to sign up, set its location/hours/serviceable area, and publish a menu (items, prices, photos, availability).
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "A restaurant owner can put their shop on the app, list their dishes with prices, and flip an item to 'sold out' when they run out — and customers see that change quickly."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** menus change constantly (prices, sold-out toggles) and the *write* path (owner edits) and the *read* path (millions browsing) are wildly imbalanced, so the catalog has to fan out to a cache and a search index without the owner waiting.

**2. Search & discovery** — A user should be able to search ("biryani near me") and browse ranked, *serviceable* restaurants/dishes, filtered by location, cuisine, rating, price, and ETA.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Someone types 'pizza' or just opens the app, and within a second they see open restaurants near them that can actually deliver to their address, sorted by what's most relevant."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** every result must be filtered by *geo serviceability* AND ranked by relevance across hundreds of thousands of restaurants in <200ms — no plain SQL `LIKE` can do geo + full-text + ranking at that latency.

**3. Order placement & payment** — A user should be able to build a cart, place an order, pay, and get a confirmed order with an ETA; the restaurant should receive it.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "I tap 'Place order', my card gets charged once, the restaurant gets a ticket, and I get an order number — and if my phone dies mid-tap I'm never charged twice."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** this is *money* — it must be atomic and exactly-once across the user's wallet/card, the restaurant's acceptance, and a third-party payment gateway we don't control; a retry must not double-charge.

**4. Delivery assignment & real-time tracking** — After an order is accepted, the system should assign a nearby rider and stream the rider's live location to the user until delivery.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Once the restaurant accepts, the app finds a delivery person near the restaurant, and I can watch their dot move on the map toward me in real time."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** assignment is a live geospatial optimization (nearest free rider, batching, ETA), and tracking is a *firehose* — hundreds of thousands of riders pinging GPS every few seconds (~100K writes/s at peak) that must reach the right viewers with sub-second latency.

**5. Notifications** — The system should push status updates (order accepted, rider assigned, out for delivery, delivered) to users, restaurants, and riders.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Everyone involved gets a buzz on their phone at each step — 'your order is on the way' — without the app having to constantly ask."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** notifications are event-driven and multi-channel (push/SMS/in-app), must be at-least-once but deduped, and a slow notification provider must never block the order or tracking path.

**Below the line (out of scope for today):** ratings & reviews ingestion, surge/dynamic pricing engine, coupons/loyalty, fraud/risk scoring, customer support tooling, rider payouts/settlement, and the recommendation-model training pipeline. I'd build the system so these bolt on as event consumers later.

### Non-Functional

**Availability** — The discovery + order path should be **99.95%** available; the money/payment write path **99.99%**.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "99.95% means under ~4.5 hours of downtime a year for browsing; 99.99% means under ~1 hour a year for the part that touches money."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** if discovery is down at 8pm dinner peak, we lose the entire day's revenue window; if the order path drops a confirmed-and-paid order, a customer is charged with no food coming — a trust-killer and a chargeback.

**Latency** — Search/feed **p99 < 200ms**; order placement confirmation **p99 < 1s**; live-location update visible to the viewer **p99 < 2s** end-to-end.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Search feels instant, placing an order feels snappy, and the rider's dot is never more than ~2 seconds behind where they really are."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** a 2-second search makes users bounce to a competitor; a laggy map makes the customer call the rider, which floods support.

**Scalability** — Must scale horizontally to **~5M orders/day**, **~35K feed reads/s** and **~100K location pings/s** at peak, growing with cities.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "When we add a new city or it's New Year's Eve, we just add machines — nothing needs a rewrite."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** a vertically-scaled tracking DB melts at dinner peak and every live map in the country freezes at once.

**Consistency** — *Strong* for money + inventory/acceptance; *eventual* (seconds) is fine for catalog display, search, and the live map.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "We're strict where it's money, relaxed where a few seconds of staleness on a menu or a moving dot doesn't matter."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** if we made the map strongly consistent we'd pay a huge latency tax for no benefit; if we made payments eventually consistent we'd double-charge people.

**Durability** — Orders/payments **RPO ≈ 0** (lose nothing on a crash). Location pings are ephemeral — losing a few seconds of dots is fine.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "A confirmed order can never be lost even if a data center dies; a few dropped GPS points don't matter."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** losing an in-flight paid order is unrecoverable trust damage and a financial liability.

> 💬 "The whole design is really about *separating three workloads that fight each other* — reads, money, and geo — onto stores that fit each, then stitching them with an event stream."

> ⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** exactly-once on the third-party payment gateway is impossible to *guarantee* end to end — the gateway can succeed while our ack is lost — so I'll get to *effectively-once* with idempotency keys + reconciliation, and I'll call that out in the trade-off ledger rather than pretend it's solved.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "So at the core, Zomato is three systems in a trench coat: a read-heavy discovery engine, a money-correct order system, and a real-time geo system for delivery. My functional flows are onboarding a restaurant and its menu, search and discovery, placing and paying for an order, assigning a rider and tracking them live, and notifications. Non-functionally I care most about: search under 200 milliseconds, the order path being money-correct with four nines and no double-charge, and the live map staying within two seconds of reality even at a hundred thousand location pings a second. I'll be strict where it's money and relaxed where it's display — that single principle drives almost every choice I make."

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

**Q1 — "Is this a single country / a few metros, or truly global from day one?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "It decides whether I need multi-region active-active and cross-region data laws, or whether one region with multiple availability zones is plenty — that's a big complexity fork."
- ↔️ **Design fork:**
  - If GLOBAL → geo-shard by city/region, region-local writes, data residency per country.
  - If SINGLE-COUNTRY (e.g. India) → one primary region, multi-AZ, city-based sharding inside it.
- **Assumption I'll proceed with:** one country, multiple metros, multi-AZ; I'll note where multi-region would slot in.

**Q2 — "Roughly what daily order volume and DAU should I design for?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "5 million orders a day versus 50 million changes whether a single Postgres cluster suffices or I must shard aggressively from the start."
- ↔️ **Design fork:**
  - If ~5M/day → sharded Postgres for orders is comfortable; reads are the real load.
  - If ~50M/day → orders themselves need heavier sharding and the matching engine becomes the bottleneck.
- **Assumption I'll proceed with:** ~5M orders/day, ~20M DAU, ~500K restaurants.

**Q3 — "How fresh must the live rider location be — best-effort, or a hard SLA?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "If 'within a couple seconds' is fine I can use a pub/sub fan-out and never touch a database on the hot path; if it must be exact-to-the-meter I need a very different, costlier pipeline."
- ↔️ **Design fork:**
  - If ~2s best-effort → in-memory geo + pub/sub, no DB write per ping.
  - If sub-second guaranteed → edge brokers per city + dedicated streaming.
- **Assumption I'll proceed with:** ~2s p99, best-effort; dropping occasional pings is acceptable.

**Q4 — "Do we need to support scheduled/pre-orders and order modification after placement?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Modification-after-payment turns a simple state machine into a saga with refunds; I want to know if that's in scope."
- ↔️ **Design fork:**
  - If YES → order state machine needs amend/partial-refund transitions + re-pricing.
  - If NO → order is immutable once paid; cancel = full refund only.
- **Assumption I'll proceed with:** immediate orders only, cancel-before-accept = full refund; modification out of scope.

**Q5 — "Is delivery first-party riders only, or also restaurant self-delivery / third-party fleets?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "It changes whether assignment is fully ours to optimize or partly delegated, which reshapes the matching service and the tracking source."
- ↔️ **Design fork:**
  - If FIRST-PARTY ONLY → we own rider locations and the matching engine end to end.
  - If MIXED → matching must federate to partner APIs and ingest their tracking.
- **Assumption I'll proceed with:** first-party rider fleet; partner fleets are a future event-consumer.

**Q6 — "How strict is payment — do we own a wallet, or only pass through a gateway like Razorpay/Stripe?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "If we hold a wallet balance I need ACID ledger semantics in-house; if it's pass-through, the hard part is idempotency and reconciliation against the gateway."
- ↔️ **Design fork:**
  - If WALLET → in-house double-entry ledger with strong consistency.
  - If PASS-THROUGH → idempotency keys + webhook reconciliation with the gateway.
- **Assumption I'll proceed with:** pass-through gateway + a thin wallet for refunds/credits.

**Q7 — "What's the read:write shape — is discovery the dominant load?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "If reads dominate by 100:1, I spend my whole latency/cost budget on caching and the search tier, not on the write path."
- ↔️ **Design fork:**
  - If read-dominant → invest in CDN/cache/search replicas.
  - If write-dominant → invest in order sharding throughput.
- **Assumption I'll proceed with:** ~120:1 read:write — discovery is the dominant load.

> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Those are my assumptions — single country, ~5M orders/day, first-party riders, pass-through payments, ~2-second live tracking. Anything there you want to flip before I size it?"

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

I computed these exactly (ran the arithmetic) and rounded to say-out-loud numbers. *Gloss: **QPS** = queries per second, how many requests hit the servers each second; **DAU** = daily active users; **TTL** = time-to-live, how long a cached value lives before it expires.*

| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Orders/sec (peak) | 5M orders/day ÷ 86,400s ≈ 58/s avg; dinner peak ~4x → 58 × 4 ≈ 231/s | ~230/s | "a couple hundred orders a second at dinner" | Order write path is *modest* — money correctness matters more than throughput |
| Feed reads/sec (peak) | 20M DAU × 30 reads/day = 600M/day ÷ 86,400 ≈ 6.9K/s avg; peak ~5x → ~35K/s | ~35K/s | "about thirty-five thousand reads a second" | Cache + search replicas absorb this; Postgres must never see it |
| Read : write ratio | 600M reads ÷ 5M orders ≈ 120 | ~120:1 | "roughly a hundred-to-one reads to writes" | Spend the budget on the *read* tier |
| Concurrent deliveries (peak) | 230 orders/s × 30-min fulfillment (1,800s) ≈ 416K live at once | ~400K | "around four hundred thousand active deliveries at peak" | Sizes the tracking/matching tier |
| **Location pings/sec (peak)** | 400K riders × 1 ping / 4s ≈ 104K/s | **~100K/s** | "a hundred thousand GPS pings a second" | **THE binding number** — never write these to a disk DB per ping |
| Orders storage/year | 5M/day × 365 × ~1KB/row × 5 (indexes+replicas+WAL) ≈ 9TB/yr | ~9TB/yr | "under ten terabytes a year" | Storage is a *non-issue* for orders — sharded Postgres is fine |
| Location data/day | 100K/s × 86,400 × ~50B ≈ 450GB/day | ~450GB/day | "about half a terabyte a day of dots" | Keep ephemeral (Redis/TTL), archive sampled to cold storage only |

**The one number that forces the design: ~100K location pings/s.** A single Redis node handles ~100–150K ops/s, so we're at the ceiling of *one* node for the whole country — and that's before fan-out to viewers. **Flip threshold:** below ~50K/s a single regional Redis geo-index works; above it we **shard the location store by city/geohash** and fan out via per-city pub/sub channels. Storage, by contrast, is boring (~9TB/yr for orders) — a non-issue.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The numbers that actually drive this: reads beat writes about a hundred to one — roughly thirty-five thousand discovery reads a second versus a couple hundred orders — so I'll spend my budget on caching and search, not the write path. But the *real* monster is location: about a hundred thousand GPS pings a second at peak from four hundred thousand active riders. That single number says I can't touch a disk database per ping — it has to be in-memory and sharded by city. Everything else, including storage, is comfortable."

## 4. Core Entities

This is a first draft of the entities — I'll add full field definitions in §7 (Data Model).

- **Restaurant** — owns its profile, location, hours, serviceable area, and rating. The unit that gets searched and ordered from.
  - 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "Think of it like a store page — its address, opening hours, and whether it's currently open and reachable from your door."
  - ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "How do you know a restaurant can deliver to me?" → it carries a serviceable-area polygon / radius checked against the user's geohash at search time.

- **MenuItem** — owns a dish: name, price, photo, availability, and which restaurant it belongs to. The thing that's added to a cart.
  - 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "A line on a paper menu — a dish with a price and a little 'sold out' sticker the owner can slap on."
  - ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "What happens when an owner marks it sold-out mid-rush?" → the write goes to the catalog DB and is propagated via CDC to cache + search within seconds; carts re-validate at checkout.

- **User** — the customer: profile, saved addresses, payment tokens, order history.
  - 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "Your account — your saved home/work addresses and your saved cards (as tokens, never raw numbers)."
  - ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "Where's the price decided?" → server-side at checkout from the catalog, never trusted from the client.

- **Order** — the central transaction: user, restaurant, line items, total, status, assigned rider, timestamps. Drives the whole state machine.
  - 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "A receipt that's also a to-do list — it starts as 'pending', moves through 'accepted → preparing → out for delivery → delivered', and never skips a step."
  - ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "Can the same order be placed twice on a retry?" → an idempotency key on placement dedupes retries to one order.

- **Payment** — the money record tied to an order: amount, gateway reference, status, idempotency key.
  - 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "The charge slip — proof we asked the bank for money exactly once and what it said back."
  - ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "Gateway succeeds but your server crashes — now what?" → the idempotency key + a reconciliation job match the gateway's webhook to our record; no double charge.

- **Rider (DeliveryPartner)** — a delivery person: current location, online/offline, current assignment, vehicle.
  - 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "A driver's live pin on the map plus a flag for 'free' or 'busy'."
  - ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "Where do you store the location?" → in an in-memory geo-index (Redis), not the relational DB — it changes every few seconds.

- **Assignment** — the link between an Order and a Rider, with state (offered/accepted/picked-up/delivered).
  - 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "The hand-off ticket that says 'rider #42 is taking order #999', and tracks whether they accepted."
  - ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "Two orders offered to one rider at once?" → the offer takes a short lock/lease on the rider; the loser is re-offered elsewhere.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The entities are Restaurant, MenuItem, User, Order, Payment, Rider, and Assignment. The two that carry the most weight are the Order — that's the state machine the whole system revolves around — and the Rider's location, which is special because it's not really database data, it's a firehose of GPS that lives in memory. I'll give every one of these full fields, types, and a home store in the data model section."

## 5. API / Interface

Default is **REST over HTTPS**; the live-tracking channel is the one justified exception (WebSocket/SSE). Security rule throughout: **the caller is identified from a JWT** *(JSON Web Token — a signed, tamper-proof token the client sends to prove who it is)*, and we **never trust client-supplied prices, user ids, or timestamps** — those come from the server/DB.

**1. Onboard / update menu** (req #1)
- `POST /v1/restaurants/{id}/menu` — POST because it creates/updates menu state on the server.
- Key request fields: `items[]: {name, price, photoUrl, available}` — the menu payload. `version: int` — optimistic-concurrency guard so two simultaneous edits don't clobber each other. **Absent:** no `restaurantId` in the body — it's in the path and authorized against the JWT, so an owner can't edit someone else's menu.
```
// Request
POST /v1/restaurants/r_123/menu
{ "version": 7, "items": [ {"id":"m_9","name":"Paneer Biryani","price":280,"available":true} ] }

// Response 200
{ "version": 8, "updatedItems": 1, "propagation": "queued" }
```
- 🔑 **Design decision:** the response says `propagation: queued` — the owner's write is acknowledged immediately and fan-out to cache + search index is async via CDC, so the owner never waits on the read tier.

**2. Search / discovery** (req #2)
- `GET /v1/search?q=biryani&lat=..&lng=..&filters=..` — GET because it only reads.
- Key request fields: `lat,lng: float` — for geo serviceability + distance ranking; `q: string` — full-text query; `filters` — cuisine/rating/price. **Absent:** no userId in the query string — taken from JWT for personalization.
```
// Request
GET /v1/search?q=biryani&lat=12.97&lng=77.59&sort=relevance

// Response 200
{ "results": [ {"restaurantId":"r_123","name":"Biryani House","etaMins":32,"rating":4.4,"deliverable":true} ], "tookMs": 41 }
```
- 🔑 **Design decision:** every result is pre-filtered by *deliverability* (serviceable to this lat/lng) at the search tier so the client never shows a restaurant that can't deliver.

**3. Place order + pay** (req #3)
- `POST /v1/orders` — POST creates new order state.
- Key request fields: `cartId: string` — server re-prices from the catalog; `paymentMethodId: string` — a *token*, never a raw card (keeps us out of PCI scope); `idempotencyKey: string` — client-generated UUID so a retry maps to the same order. **Absent:** no `price`/`total` — the server computes it from the catalog so the client can't tamper with the amount.
```
// Request
POST /v1/orders
Idempotency-Key: 9f1c-..-uuid
{ "cartId": "cart_77", "addressId": "addr_3", "paymentMethodId": "pm_tok_abc" }

// Response 202
{ "orderId": "ord_555", "status": "PENDING_PAYMENT", "etaMins": 38 }
```
- 🔑 **Design decision:** returns **202 + PENDING_PAYMENT**, not a synchronous "PAID". `status` is server-set (client can't forge it); payment settles asynchronously and the order advances via events — so a slow gateway doesn't hang the user's request.

**4a. Rider location ingest** (req #4)
- `POST /v1/riders/{id}/location` (or a persistent WebSocket frame) — high-frequency write, fire-and-forget.
- Key request fields: `lat,lng: float`, `ts: epoch`, `accuracy: float`. **Absent:** no order context — the system already knows the rider's current assignment.
- 🔑 **Design decision:** this endpoint **does not hit a relational DB** — it updates an in-memory geo index and publishes to the order's tracking channel. At ~100K/s, durability is deliberately traded for speed.

**4b. Track order (live)** (req #4)
- `GET /v1/orders/{id}/track` upgraded to **WebSocket/SSE** — a push channel, because polling 400K live orders would hammer the server.
```
// Server → client frames
{ "orderId":"ord_555","riderLat":12.96,"riderLng":77.60,"etaMins":11,"status":"OUT_FOR_DELIVERY" }
```
- 🔑 **Design decision:** WebSocket over polling — the viewer subscribes once and the server pushes ~every 2s; this is the only place we leave REST.

**5. Notifications** (req #5) — internal, event-driven; no public endpoint. A `notification.requested` event is emitted by the order state machine and consumed by a Notification service. (Webhook in: `POST /v1/webhooks/payment` from the gateway, signature-verified.)

> 💬 "Notice the two non-obvious calls: place-order returns 202 PENDING, not a synchronous charge; and location ingest skips the database entirely."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The APIs map one-to-one to the flows. Menu publish is a POST that's acknowledged instantly and propagates async. Search is a plain GET, geo-filtered so you never see a restaurant that can't reach you. Place-order is a POST with an idempotency key that returns 202 pending — the charge happens asynchronously so a slow bank never freezes the app, and the price is computed server-side so it can't be tampered with. Location ingest is a firehose write that deliberately never touches the relational database, and tracking is the one WebSocket in the design because pushing beats polling for hundreds of thousands of live maps."

## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time)

I'll define the color palette once and reuse it: blue = clients, green = our services, amber = queues/streams, grey = datastores, purple = external.

### 6.1 "Restaurant onboarding & menu management"

The owner writes rarely; millions read constantly. The whole trick is making one slow write fan out to a fast read tier without the owner waiting.

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Catalog DB | PostgreSQL — a relational database storing restaurants/menus in rows with strict schema | Menus are structured, relational (restaurant→items), and need transactional edits + a source of truth | Not MongoDB (we don't need schemaless and want one source of truth); not a KV store (can't query/relate) | Must fan out separately to read tier |
| CDC pipeline | Change Data Capture via Debezium+Kafka — streams every row change out of Postgres as an event | Lets cache + search update within seconds of an edit, decoupled from the write | Not dual-writes from the app (race conditions, partial failures); not a cron re-index (minutes stale) | A few seconds of propagation lag |
| Search index | Elasticsearch — a search engine that pre-builds a word→restaurant map for ranked geo+text queries | Need full-text + geo + ranking in <100ms; SQL can't | Not SQL `LIKE` (scans every row, no ranking); not hand-rolled index (months of work) | Index lags catalog by a few seconds (fine) |
| Read cache | Redis — an in-memory key-value store for hot restaurant/menu pages | Absorbs the ~35K reads/s so Postgres never sees browse traffic | Not "just query Postgres" (melts under read load) | Cache invalidation on edits via CDC |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "I store the catalog in Postgres because a menu is structured, relational data and I want one transactional source of truth. The moment an owner edits, I don't want them waiting on search or cache, so I capture the change with CDC — Change Data Capture, which just means streaming every database row-change out as an event — and let downstream systems update themselves. Elasticsearch handles search because I need geo plus full-text plus ranking in under a hundred milliseconds, which SQL simply can't do. Redis caches hot pages so Postgres never feels the browse load. The trade-off is a few seconds of staleness on the read side, which is totally fine for a menu."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **PostgreSQL** — an open-source relational database with rows, columns, and transactions. Here it's the single source of truth for restaurants and menus.
> **CDC (Change Data Capture)** — a technique that streams every insert/update/delete out of a database as an event. Here it propagates menu edits to cache + search without the app dual-writing.
> **Debezium** — a CDC tool that reads Postgres's write-ahead log and emits change events to Kafka. Here it's the plumbing that turns a menu edit into a stream.
> **Elasticsearch** — a search engine that pre-indexes text and geo data for fast ranked queries. Here it powers search and discovery.
> **Redis** — an in-memory key-value store, microsecond reads. Here it caches hot restaurant/menu pages.

**Menu publish + fan-out**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Owner[Restaurant app]
    Owner -->|"1: POST menu"| Gateway[API Gateway]
    Gateway -->|"2: write"| Catalog[(Postgres catalog)]
    Catalog -.->|"3: CDC change event"| Stream[[Kafka menu-changes]]
    Stream -.->|"4: index"| ES[(Elasticsearch)]
    Stream -.->|"5: invalidate"| Cache[(Redis cache)]
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    class Owner client
    class Gateway svc
    class Stream queue
    class Catalog,ES,Cache db
```
↳ reuses existing: none (this is the foundation slice).

**Numbered step narration:**
1. **Owner POSTs the menu** → API Gateway authenticates the JWT and authorizes the owner against `restaurantId`. *State:* nothing yet. *What if* the JWT is for another restaurant → 403, rejected.
2. **Gateway writes to Postgres** with an optimistic `version` check. *Why here:* Postgres is the transactional source of truth. *State:* the row is updated, version bumped. *What if* two edits race → the stale `version` loses and retries.
3. **CDC emits a change event** to Kafka. *Why:* decouples the slow fan-out from the owner's request. *State:* event durably buffered. *What if* a consumer is down → Kafka retains the event until it recovers.
4. **Elasticsearch consumes and re-indexes** the changed items. *State:* search reflects the new price/availability within seconds. *What if* indexing fails → retried from Kafka offset; search is briefly stale, not wrong-forever.
5. **Redis cache is invalidated** for that restaurant's keys. *State:* next reader repopulates from Postgres. *What if* invalidation is missed → TTL (60s) bounds staleness as a safety net.

> 💬 "The key insight: the owner's write is acknowledged the instant it hits Postgres — everything downstream is async, so publishing a menu is never slow."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Onboarding is the foundation. Postgres holds the catalog as the source of truth. When an owner edits, I acknowledge immediately and let Change Data Capture stream that change into Kafka, which fans out to Elasticsearch for search and invalidates Redis for caching. The one decision that makes this work is *async fan-out* — the owner never waits on the read tier — and the trade-off, a few seconds of propagation lag, is invisible for a menu."

### 6.2 "Search & discovery"

The hot read path. A user opens the app or searches, and we must return ranked, *deliverable* results in <200ms across 500K restaurants.

| Component | What it is | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Search service | Stateless service querying Elasticsearch with geo+text+filters | Owns ranking logic + deliverability filter | Not querying ES from the client (security, no business logic) | Extra hop (sub-ms) |
| Geo filter | Geohash/`geo_distance` in ES + serviceable-area check | Must exclude non-deliverable restaurants pre-ranking | Not post-filtering in the app (wastes ranking on undeliverable rows) | Index must carry geo |
| CDN/edge cache | Caches popular *anonymous* feeds (e.g. "open now in area X") | Cuts repeated identical browse queries near the edge | Not caching personalized results (low hit rate) | Only helps non-personalized feeds |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "Search is a thin stateless service in front of Elasticsearch. It does two things SQL can't: full-text ranking and a geo-distance filter, so I only ever rank restaurants that can actually deliver to your pin. For the common anonymous 'what's open near me' feed, I cache at the CDN edge to shave repeated work. I don't let the client hit Elasticsearch directly — that would leak the index and skip my business logic."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Geohash** — encoding a lat/lng into a short string so nearby points share a prefix. Here it makes 'restaurants near me' a fast prefix lookup.
> **CDN (Content Delivery Network)** — a network of edge servers near users that cache content. Here it caches anonymous browse feeds and all the restaurant images.

**Search read path**

```mermaid
    Gateway -->|"2: route"| Search[Search service]
    Search -->|"3: geo+text query"| ES[(Elasticsearch)]
    Search -.->|"4: hot feed"| Cache[(Redis cache)]
    ES -->|"5: ranked hits"| Search
    Search -->|"6: results"| User

    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    class User client
```

↳ reuses existing: Elasticsearch, Redis (from §6.1).

**Numbered step narration:**
1. **User issues search** with lat/lng. *State:* none. *What if* no lat/lng → fall back to saved address from profile.
2. **Gateway routes to Search service.** *Why:* central authn + rate-limiting. *What if* abusive client → rate-limited at the gateway.
3. **Search queries Elasticsearch** with combined geo-distance + full-text + filters. *Why:* only ES does ranked geo+text fast. *State:* none (read). *What if* ES is slow → serve a cached/degraded feed.
4. **Hot anonymous feeds** served from Redis/CDN. *State:* none. *What if* miss → fall through to ES.
5–6. **ES returns ranked hits → Search returns results.** *What if* a result's restaurant just closed → client re-validates open-status at cart time.

> 💬 "I rank only deliverable restaurants — geo-filter *before* ranking, never after."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Discovery is read-heavy, so it lives entirely on Elasticsearch and caches — Postgres never sees a browse. The Search service combines a geo-distance filter with full-text ranking so you only ever see restaurants that can actually deliver to you, ranked by relevance and ETA. The trade-off is that search can lag the catalog by a few seconds, which is fine for display. If Elasticsearch hiccups, I degrade to a cached 'popular nearby' feed rather than fail."

### 6.3 "Order placement & payment"

The money path. This must be atomic and exactly-once across order creation, payment, and restaurant acceptance — even under retries.

| Component | What it is | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Order service | Stateless service owning the order state machine, writes to Postgres | Orders need ACID + a strict status lifecycle | Not eventual store (can't risk a lost/partial order) | Must shard as volume grows |
| Order DB | Sharded PostgreSQL, sharded by `order_id` | ACID for money; row locks prevent double-spend | Not DynamoDB (no easy multi-row ACID saga); not Mongo (weaker txns here) | Cross-shard joins avoided by design |
| Idempotency store | Redis with the client's `idempotencyKey` → orderId, TTL 24h | Dedupes retries to exactly one order | Not "just check the DB" (slower, racy) | Extra store to operate |
| Payment service | Calls external gateway; reconciles via webhook | Isolates the unreliable third party from the order path | Not calling gateway inline in the request (slow, blocks user) | Async settlement (PENDING state) |
| Order events | Kafka `order-events` stream | Drives notifications, matching, analytics off one log | Not synchronous calls to each (tight coupling, cascading failure) | At-least-once → consumers must dedupe |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "Orders go to Postgres because this is money and I need ACID — a transaction that either fully commits or fully rolls back. I shard by order_id so the write tier scales horizontally and each shard is independent. Before I even create an order I check an idempotency key in Redis, so a user mashing 'Place order' or a network retry produces exactly one order, not three. Payment is *not* inline — I call the gateway and settle asynchronously, returning a PENDING order, so a slow bank never freezes the app. Everything else — notifying the restaurant, kicking off matching — hangs off a Kafka order-events log so the components stay decoupled."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **ACID** — Atomicity/Consistency/Isolation/Durability: a transaction fully commits or fully rolls back. Here it guarantees we never half-charge or half-record an order.
> **Idempotency key** — a unique token attached to a request so retries are recognized and not re-executed. Here it stops double orders/charges.
> **Saga** — a sequence of local transactions with compensating undo steps, used when one big distributed transaction isn't possible. Here it coordinates order→payment→acceptance with refunds as compensation.
> **Kafka** — a durable, replayable event log that buffers a firehose of events. Here it's the backbone that fans order events out to notifications, matching, and analytics.

**Order + payment flow**
```mermaid

    User[User app]
    User -->|"1: POST order +idemKey"| Gateway[API Gateway]
    Gateway -->|"2: dedupe"| Idem[(Redis idempotency)]
    Gateway -->|"3: create PENDING"| Order[Order service]
    Order -->|"4: write txn"| ODB[(Sharded Postgres)]
    Order -.->|"5: charge"| Pay[Payment service]
    Pay -.->|"6: authorize"| GW[External gateway]
    GW -.->|"7: webhook PAID"| Pay
    Pay -.->|"8: order.paid"| Events[[Kafka order-events]]
    Events -.->|"9: ticket"| RApp[Restaurant app]
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
```

↳ reuses existing: Kafka (from §6.1), Redis.

**Numbered step narration:**
1. **User POSTs order** with an idempotency key. *State:* none yet.
2. **Gateway checks Redis** for that key. *What if* key already exists → return the *same* orderId, no new order (this is the dedup).
3–4. **Order service creates a PENDING order** in a Postgres transaction, re-pricing server-side from the catalog. *State:* order persisted as PENDING_PAYMENT. *What if* crash here → no charge yet; client retry hits the same idempotency key.
5–6. **Payment service authorizes** with the external gateway, passing the same idempotency key downstream. *What if* gateway times out → we don't double-charge; reconciliation resolves it.
7. **Gateway webhook confirms PAID** (signature-verified). *State:* payment recorded; order → CONFIRMED. *What if* webhook lost → a reconciliation job polls the gateway and self-heals.
8–9. **`order.paid` event** flows through Kafka to the restaurant app (a ticket) and to matching. *State:* restaurant sees the order. *What if* a consumer is down → Kafka replays when it returns.

> 💬 "The idempotency key is the hero here — it's what makes a retry safe, so a flaky network never double-charges anyone."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Placing an order is the money path, so it's the strict part. I check an idempotency key first so retries can't create duplicate orders, then write a PENDING order in a Postgres transaction with the price computed server-side. Payment runs asynchronously against the gateway — I never block the user on a bank call — and a signed webhook flips the order to confirmed, with a reconciliation job as a safety net if that webhook is ever lost. Once paid, a single Kafka event fans out to the restaurant and the matching engine. The trade-off is that payment is eventually-confirmed rather than instant, which I accept because the alternative — a synchronous charge — freezes the app whenever the bank is slow."

### 6.4 "Delivery assignment & real-time tracking"

Two hard things at once: pick the best nearby rider (live geo optimization), then stream their dot to the user at ~100K pings/s without melting a database.

| Component | What it is | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Matching service | Service that finds nearest free riders and offers the order | Owns assignment logic + ETA | Not random/round-robin (terrible ETAs) | Compute-heavy at peak |
| Location store | Redis geo-index (GEOADD/GEOSEARCH), sharded by city | In-memory geo radius query in ms; absorbs 100K writes/s | Not Postgres/PostGIS (can't take 100K writes/s of churn) | Ephemeral — pings can be lost |
| Tracking fan-out | Pub/Sub channels per order; WebSocket gateway pushes to viewers | Push beats polling 400K live maps | Not client polling (35K+ extra QPS, laggy) | Sticky connections to manage |
| Location ingest | Lightweight ingest that updates geo-index + publishes | Keeps the firehose off the relational DB | Not writing each ping to disk (450GB/day, pointless) | No long-term ping history (sampled only) |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "Assignment is a live geospatial problem: when an order is paid, the Matching service does a radius search over an in-memory Redis geo-index to find free riders near the restaurant, scores them by ETA, and offers the order — taking a short lease on the chosen rider so two orders can't grab the same person. Tracking is the firehose: riders ping GPS every few seconds, about a hundred thousand a second at peak. I never write those to a disk database — I update the Redis geo-index and publish to a per-order channel, and a WebSocket gateway pushes the dot to whoever's watching. The trade-off is that location is best-effort and ephemeral, which is exactly right — nobody cares about a GPS point from ten minutes ago."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Redis geo-index (GEOADD/GEOSEARCH)** — Redis commands that store points by lat/lng and find all points within a radius in milliseconds. Here it holds live rider locations and powers 'nearest free rider'.
> **Pub/Sub** — publish/subscribe: publishers send messages to a channel, subscribers receive them. Here each order has a channel that viewers subscribe to for live dots.
> **WebSocket** — a persistent two-way connection between client and server. Here it lets the server *push* location updates instead of the client polling.
> **Lease/lock** — a short-lived exclusive claim on a resource. Here it reserves a rider during an offer so no double-assignment.

**Assignment + tracking**

```mermaid
    Ingest -->|"2: GEOADD"| Geo[(Redis geo-index)]
    Ingest -.->|"3: publish dot"| PubSub[[Pub/Sub channel]]
    Paid[[Kafka order.paid]] -.->|"4: match"| Match[Matching service]
    Match -->|"5: GEOSEARCH nearby"| Geo
    Match -->|"6: offer + lease"| Rider
    PubSub -.->|"7: push"| WS[WebSocket gateway]
    WS -.->|"8: live dot"| Viewer
```

↳ reuses existing: Kafka order-events (from §6.3).

**Numbered step narration:**
1–2. **Rider pings location → ingest GEOADDs** into the city-sharded Redis geo-index. *State:* rider's pin updated. *What if* a ping is dropped → next ping (≤4s) corrects it; no harm.
3. **Ingest publishes** the dot to the order's channel. *What if* no subscriber → message discarded (nobody's watching).
4–5. **`order.paid` triggers Matching**, which GEOSEARCHes free riders near the restaurant. *State:* candidate list. *What if* none free → retry with widening radius / queue the order.
6. **Matching offers + leases** the best rider. *State:* rider held; Assignment created. *What if* rider declines/times out → lease expires, re-offer to next.
7–8. **Pub/Sub → WebSocket gateway pushes** the live dot to the viewer. *State:* user sees movement. *What if* WS drops → client reconnects and resubscribes; map resumes within seconds.

> 💬 "The whole trick: location lives in memory and flows through pub/sub — it never becomes a database write, which is the only way 100K pings a second is affordable."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Once an order is paid, the matching engine does an in-memory radius search over rider locations, scores by ETA, and offers the order with a short lease so two orders can't grab one rider. Tracking is the part people get wrong: a hundred thousand GPS pings a second can't go to a disk database, so I keep rider locations in a Redis geo-index sharded by city and fan the dots out through pub/sub to WebSocket connections. The trade-off — location is ephemeral and best-effort — is the right call, because a stale GPS point is worthless and the next ping fixes it in seconds."

### 6.5 "Notifications"

Every state change should buzz the right people, multi-channel, without ever blocking the order or tracking path.

| Component | What it is | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Notification service | Kafka consumer of `order-events` → push/SMS/email | Fully decoupled; can't slow the order path | Not synchronous calls from Order service (couples + cascades failures) | At-least-once → must dedupe |
| Channel providers | FCM/APNs (push), SMS gateway, email | Right channel per platform | Not building delivery infra ourselves | Vendor dependency |
| Dedup store | Redis set of sent `(eventId,channel)` | Stops duplicate buzzes from replays | Not nothing (Kafka is at-least-once) | Small extra store |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "Notifications are a pure consumer of the order-events log — they hang completely off to the side, so even if the push provider is slow, orders and tracking are unaffected. Because Kafka is at-least-once I dedupe on event-id-plus-channel in Redis so a replay doesn't double-buzz you. I fan to FCM and APNs for push, plus SMS as a fallback."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **FCM / APNs** — Firebase Cloud Messaging / Apple Push Notification service: the OS-level pipes that deliver push notifications to Android/iOS. Here they're the last hop to the user's phone.
> **At-least-once** — a delivery guarantee where a message may be delivered more than once but never lost. Here it's why we dedupe.

> 💬 "Notifications live entirely off the event log, so the slowest external provider can never slow down an order."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Notifications are the cleanest slice: a consumer of the order-events log, completely decoupled, so a slow push provider can't touch the order path. I dedupe on event id and channel because Kafka is at-least-once, then fan out to FCM, APNs, and SMS. If push fails for a critical status I fall back to SMS."
